title: Deep litter bedding management for a rabbit colony
subtitle: Building up and maintaining a composting bedding system.
excerpt: Deep litter bedding (where fresh material is added over existing bedding rather than full cleaning-out at each interval) provides warmth, microbial competition with pathogens, and constant environmental enrichment for a rabbit colony. Managed correctly it produces excellent composted manure and reduces labour. Managed incorrectly it becomes a damp, ammonia-laden disease reservoir.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits (2019). Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education (SARE) guides on deep litter systems for small livestock. RWAF housing guidance, rwaf.org.uk.

glossaryTerms.definition: A housing management approach where new bedding material is added on top of existing material at frequent intervals, building up a composting layer beneath the animals. The aerobic microbial activity in the lower layers generates warmth and suppresses pathogens, provided the system remains aerobic (not saturated).

content.text: works best in an outdoor or semi-outdoor colony pen with a hard floor, a roof, and enough airflow to stop the surface layer getting wet. It is not suitable for hutches where waste builds up in a tight space. Hutches need regular full cleaning.

Lay a base of 10 to 15 cm of straw or wood shavings before introducing the rabbits. Straw provides better composting carbon structure than shavings alone; a mix of both works well. The first weeks produce little visible breakdown; the composting process establishes over 4 to 6 weeks as the microbial population builds.

Add 2 to 5 cm of fresh straw twice a week, or whenever the surface appears wet or the ammonia smell becomes noticeable at floor level. The goal is to keep the surface layer dry and freely available for the rabbits to dig in. A surface that crumbles when touched and shows no visible wet patches is working correctly. A surface that clumps, holds the shape of a footprint, or produces visible moisture when compressed is too wet and needs immediate topping-up, not waiting for the next scheduled addition.

Push your hand 10 cm into the litter at several points. The lower layers should feel warm but not wet or slimy; they should smell earthy rather than of ammonia. A temperature of 30 to 40°C in the lower layers is the composting zone working correctly. If the litter smells strongly of ammonia throughout, or if the surface cannot be kept dry despite regular additions, the system has become anaerobic and needs stripping and replacing.

In a well-run system, a full clean-out is needed every 3 to 6 months. Strip the pen completely. Disinfect the floor with a product active against oocysts (potassium peroxymonosulphate compounds work; ordinary Virkon does not). Let it dry in air and sunlight. Start the litter bed fresh. Time the clean-out before a doe's kindling if possible. A freshly reset pen has the lowest disease risk for newborn kits.
